categoryName: Introduction to Pathology, Cell Injury, and Adaptation

questions.topicName: Introduction to Pathology, Cell Injury, and Adaptation
questions.stem: This H&E field from an aggressive pediatric CNS tumor shows the dark structures indicated by the arrows. What do these structures directly indicate about the tumor?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Programmed cell death of tumor cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: A high rate of cellular proliferation

explanation.summary: The arrows point to mitotic figures (condensed dividing chromosomes staining dark purple). Abundant mitoses are a morphologic marker of rapid cellular proliferation, seen here in a medulloblastoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: The arrows point to mitotic figures (condensed dividing chromosomes staining dark purple). Abundant mitoses are a morphologic marker of rapid cellular proliferation, seen here in a medulloblastoma.

questions.topicName: Introduction to Pathology, Cell Injury, and Adaptation
questions.stem: Panel A shows normal renal tubule cells; panel B shows the same cells after an early, reversible insult. What reversible change accounts for the appearance in panel B?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cellular (hydropic) swelling from failed ion pumps

explanation.summary: Depleted ATP impairs Na+/K+ pumps, causing water influx and cellular swelling with pale, eosinophilic cytoplasm. This is a hallmark of reversible injury and resolves if the stimulus is removed.
explanation.whyCorrect: Depleted ATP impairs Na+/K+ pumps, causing water influx and cellular swelling with pale, eosinophilic cytoplasm. This is a hallmark of reversible injury and resolves if the stimulus is removed.

questions.topicName: Introduction to Pathology, Cell Injury, and Adaptation
questions.stem: These injured kidney tubule cells show swelling and increased pink cytoplasm. The fading, washed-out nuclei in some cells reflect which pattern of nuclear change?

explanation.summary: Karyolysis is the fading of basophilic nuclear staining as chromatin is degraded. It is distinct from pyknosis (shrunken dark nuclei) and karyorrhexis (nuclear fragmentation), accompanying the eosinophilia and swelling of injured cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Karyolysis is the fading of basophilic nuclear staining as chromatin is degraded. It is distinct from pyknosis (shrunken dark nuclei) and karyorrhexis (nuclear fragmentation), accompanying the eosinophilia and swelling of injured cells.

questions.topicName: Introduction to Pathology, Cell Injury, and Adaptation
questions.stem: This liver from a patient who cannot properly excrete bile shows a yellow-brown pigment within cells. What is this pigment?

explanation.summary: Bilirubin is a yellow-brown pigment from heme breakdown that accumulates when bile excretion is impaired. It is distinct from hemosiderin, the iron-containing blood breakdown product.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bilirubin is a yellow-brown pigment from heme breakdown that accumulates when bile excretion is impaired. It is distinct from hemosiderin, the iron-containing blood breakdown product.

questions.topicName: Introduction to Pathology, Cell Injury, and Adaptation
questions.stem: This gross specimen shows hard, chalky white deposits on the cusps of a damaged, atherosclerotic aortic valve. Given normal systemic calcium levels, what type of calcification is this?

explanation.summary: Dystrophic calcification is calcium deposition in damaged or dying tissue under normal systemic calcium homeostasis, as in this atherosclerotic aortic valve. Metastatic calcification instead occurs in normal tissue with hypercalcemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dystrophic calcification is calcium deposition in damaged or dying tissue under normal systemic calcium homeostasis, as in this atherosclerotic aortic valve. Metastatic calcification instead occurs in normal tissue with hypercalcemia.

questions.topicName: Introduction to Pathology, Cell Injury, and Adaptation
questions.stem: This bisected uterus shows a bulky, thickened endometrial lining compared with the normal region. With excess estrogen stimulation, what adaptive change does the bulky area represent?

explanation.summary: Excess estrogen drives increased proliferation of endometrial glandular cells (hyperplasia), producing a bulky lining and a cause of abnormal uterine bleeding.
explanation.whyCorrect: Excess estrogen drives increased proliferation of endometrial glandular cells (hyperplasia), producing a bulky lining and a cause of abnormal uterine bleeding.

questions.topicName: Introduction to Pathology, Cell Injury, and Adaptation
questions.stem: This summary diagram centers on the organelle shown. When this organelle is damaged, which paired consequences drive the resulting cell injury?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased protein synthesis and decreased ROS

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased ATP and increased reactive oxygen species

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased DNA damage and increased glycolysis

explanation.summary: Mitochondrial damage lowers ATP (impairing energy-dependent functions) and raises ROS production, which together damage lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids. Failure to reverse mitochondrial dysfunction marks irreversible injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mitochondrial damage lowers ATP (impairing energy-dependent functions) and raises ROS production, which together damage lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids. Failure to reverse mitochondrial dysfunction marks irreversible injury.

questions.topicName: Introduction to Pathology, Cell Injury, and Adaptation
questions.stem: This summary schematic integrates the major mechanisms converging on a single end point. What overarching process do the depicted pathways (ATP loss, calcium rise, ROS, membrane and DNA damage) collectively produce?

explanation.summary: The figure summarizes how decreased ATP, increased cytosolic calcium, reactive oxygen species, and membrane/DNA damage act through multiple downstream effects to cause cell injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: The figure summarizes how decreased ATP, increased cytosolic calcium, reactive oxygen species, and membrane/DNA damage act through multiple downstream effects to cause cell injury.
